title: Sources of finance
summary: Every business decision eventually runs into the same question: where does the money come from, and at what cost? This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 3.2 — the internal sources of finance (retained profit, sale of assets, sale and leaseback, personal funds), the external sources (share capital, loan capital, overdrafts, trade credit, leasing, hire purchase, crowdfunding, business angels, venture capital, microfinance and government grants), the split between short-term and long-term finance, and the factors that decide which source is appropriate (cost, purpose, amount, legal structure, level of risk, and gearing/control). Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see how a supported judgement matched to purpose, amount and risk, not a memorised list, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Distinguish internal sources of finance (retained profit, sale of assets, sale and leaseback, personal funds) from external sources, and state the features, advantages and disadvantages of each.

Describe the external sources of finance — share capital, loan capital, overdrafts, trade credit, leasing, hire purchase, crowdfunding, business angels, venture capital, microfinance and government grants.
Distinguish short-term finance from long-term finance and match the term of finance to the purpose it funds.
Explain the factors that influence the choice of source of finance — cost, purpose, amount, legal structure, level of risk and gearing/control.
Recommend an appropriate source of finance for a business by applying the concepts to context and reaching a supported judgement.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Where the money comes from
simpleExplanation.summary: Finance is fuel. A business needs it to start, to run day to day, and to grow — but the money can come from very different places, and each place has its own price tag. Some finance comes from inside the business, some from outside. Some has to be repaid with interest, some never has to be repaid but costs you a slice of ownership. Choosing the wrong source is like filling a diesel engine with petrol: even the right amount of money in the wrong form can damage the business.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of paying for a car. You could dip into your own savings — that is internal finance, cheap and yours, but it drains your reserves. You could borrow from a bank and pay it back with interest — that is loan capital, quick to arrange but a fixed cost whether or not the car earns its keep. You could ask a partner to chip in and co-own it — that is share capital, no repayments but now you share the steering wheel. Or you could lease it and simply pay to use it without ever owning it. Same car, four very different deals — and the best deal depends on how much you need, what for, and how much risk and control you can live with.
steps: Work out the purpose and the amount. A small day-to-day cash gap needs a very different source from a factory that will last twenty years.
Look inside first. Can retained profit, the sale of an unused asset, sale and leaseback or the owner's personal funds cover it without paying anyone outside?
If not, choose an external source that matches. Short-term needs suit overdrafts, trade credit and short loans; long-term needs suit share capital, long-term loans, leasing and hire purchase.
Weigh cost, risk, gearing and control, then commit. The right source is the one whose term, cost and control implications fit this business's purpose and its ability to bear risk.

flashcards.back: Finance raised from within the business itself, without going to outside parties. Sources: retained profit, sale of assets, sale and leaseback and the owner's personal funds. Generally cheaper and involves no loss of control, but the amount available is limited.

Finance obtained from individuals or institutions outside the business. Sources: share capital, loan capital, overdrafts, trade credit, leasing, hire purchase, crowdfunding, business angels, venture capital, microfinance and government grants. Can raise large sums but usually carries a cost or dilutes ownership.

Profit kept in the business after tax and dividends and reinvested rather than distributed to owners. An internal, long-term source: it costs no interest and dilutes no ownership, but is only available to profitable firms and once spent cannot be used elsewhere.

Raising finance by selling assets the business no longer needs (e.g. surplus machinery or vehicles). An internal source that turns idle assets into cash, but a firm can only do it once per asset and may struggle to sell quickly at a fair price.

Selling an asset the business still uses (e.g. its premises) to a buyer and then leasing it straight back. Releases the cash tied up in the asset while keeping its use, but the business loses ownership and must pay ongoing lease charges.

Money the owner puts into the business from their own savings. A key internal source for sole traders and partnerships: cheap and immediate, but limited to the owner's own wealth and puts that wealth at risk.

Finance raised by selling shares in a company to shareholders. A long-term, external source of EQUITY finance — it never has to be repaid and pays no interest, but it dilutes ownership and control and is only available to companies (Ltd or plc), not sole traders or partnerships.

Money borrowed from a bank or other lender and repaid over a fixed term with interest. A form of DEBT finance: it keeps ownership intact but adds a fixed repayment burden, usually needs collateral, and raises the firm's gearing.

A bank facility that lets a business withdraw more than its account holds, up to an agreed limit. A short-term, flexible source for bridging cash-flow gaps — interest is charged only on the amount used, but rates are high and the bank can demand repayment on short notice.

Buying goods or services from a supplier now and paying later (commonly 30–90 days). A short-term, interest-free source of working capital, but it is limited in size, and paying late can damage supplier relationships and forfeit early-payment discounts.

Paying a regular charge to use an asset owned by a lessor, without ever owning it. Avoids a large upfront outlay and often includes maintenance, but total cost over time can exceed buying and the business never owns the asset.

Acquiring an asset by paying a deposit and then instalments, gaining OWNERSHIP once the final payment is made. Unlike leasing the business ends up owning the asset, but instalments include interest and the asset can be repossessed if payments are missed.

Raising small amounts from a large number of people, usually via an online platform, in exchange for rewards, pre-orders or equity. Can raise finance and test demand at once, but campaigns can fail publicly and may expose the idea to competitors.

Wealthy individuals who invest their own money in high-potential start-ups in exchange for equity, often adding mentoring and contacts. Provide finance and expertise, but take a share of ownership and expect high returns.

Finance from specialist firms that invest a managed pool of money into high-risk, high-growth businesses in exchange for equity and usually a say in how the firm is run. Larger sums than angels, but significant loss of control and pressure to grow fast.

Small-scale loans and financial services offered to low-income entrepreneurs who cannot access mainstream banks, funding self-employment and small enterprises. Widens access to finance but the sums are small and interest can be high.

A sum given to a business by the government, often to encourage activity such as job creation, R&D or locating in a deprived area. Does not have to be repaid, but is usually competitive, tied to strict conditions and often only covers part of a project.

The proportion of a firm's capital that comes from debt (loan capital) rather than equity. High gearing means heavy reliance on borrowing, raising fixed interest costs and financial risk if profits fall; low gearing is safer but may under-use cheap debt.

Only incorporated companies (Ltd and plc) can raise SHARE capital, because only they issue shares. A sole trader or ordinary partnership CANNOT sell shares — recommending share capital to a sole trader is a classic error.

An overdraft is short-term, flexible and repayable on demand, with interest only on what is used — suited to small cash-flow gaps. A loan is a fixed lump sum repaid over a set term with scheduled interest — suited to a specific long-lived purchase. They are not interchangeable.

quickCheck.prompt: A business reinvests profit left after tax and dividends to buy new equipment. Which type of source is this?
options: An external source, because it funds new equipment
An internal source (retained profit), generated within the business
Loan capital, because it must be repaid with interest
Share capital, because owners provided it
quickCheck.answer: An internal source (retained profit), generated within the business

quickCheck.prompt: A firm needs finance to cover a one-month gap while it waits for customers to pay. Which source best matches this need?
options: A ten-year mortgage, because it is cheap
Share capital, because it never has to be repaid
A short-term source such as an overdraft or trade credit
A government grant for building a new factory
quickCheck.answer: A short-term source such as an overdraft or trade credit

quickCheck.prompt: What is the key difference between an overdraft and a bank loan?
options: An overdraft is a fixed lump sum repaid over years; a loan is repayable on demand
An overdraft is flexible, short-term and charges interest only on the amount used; a loan is a fixed lump sum repaid over a set term with scheduled interest
A loan charges no interest while an overdraft does
There is no real difference; the terms are interchangeable
quickCheck.answer: An overdraft is flexible, short-term and charges interest only on the amount used; a loan is a fixed lump sum repaid over a set term with scheduled interest

quickCheck.prompt: A newly registered sole trader asks how to raise finance by issuing share capital. What is the correct response?
options: Share capital is ideal because it never has to be repaid
A sole trader cannot issue share capital — only incorporated companies (Ltd or plc) can sell shares; the owner should consider personal funds, a bank loan or leasing
The sole trader should become a charity first
Share capital is the cheapest option for any business
quickCheck.answer: A sole trader cannot issue share capital — only incorporated companies (Ltd or plc) can sell shares; the owner should consider personal funds, a bank loan or leasing

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement about hire purchase and leasing is correct?
options: Both mean the business never owns the asset
With hire purchase the business owns the asset after the final instalment; with leasing it only ever pays to use the asset and never owns it
Leasing always ends in ownership; hire purchase never does
Neither involves any interest or charges
quickCheck.answer: With hire purchase the business owns the asset after the final instalment; with leasing it only ever pays to use the asset and never owns it

faq.q: What is the difference between internal and external sources of finance?
faq.a: Internal finance comes from within the business itself — retained profit, the sale of assets, sale and leaseback, and the owner's own funds. It is generally cheaper and involves no loss of control, but the amount is limited by how profitable and asset-rich the business is. External finance comes from outside — banks, shareholders, suppliers, investors, the government. It can supply much larger sums, but usually costs interest, dilutes ownership, or comes with conditions. Most established businesses use a mix of both.

faq.q: Why does the difference between short-term and long-term finance matter so much?
faq.a: Because the term of the finance should match the purpose it funds. Short-term finance (up to about a year) — overdrafts, trade credit, short loans — is for day-to-day working-capital needs like paying wages or bridging a gap before customers pay. Long-term finance (several years or more) — share capital, long-term loans, leasing, hire purchase — is for major, long-lived assets like premises or machinery. Using a short-term overdraft to buy a long-term asset is a serious mismatch: the repayment falls due long before the asset has paid for itself.

faq.q: What is the difference between a business angel and a venture capitalist?
faq.a: Both provide equity finance in exchange for a share of ownership, but a business angel is a wealthy individual investing their own money, usually smaller sums into very early-stage start-ups, often adding personal mentoring. A venture capital firm manages a pooled fund of other people's money, invests larger amounts into businesses with proven high-growth potential, and typically takes a more formal role in how the business is run. Angels tend to come earlier and smaller; venture capital comes later and larger.

faq.q: Why can't a sole trader raise share capital?
faq.a: Because share capital means selling shares, and only an incorporated company (an Ltd or a plc) issues shares. A sole trader is not a separate legal entity, so there are no shares to sell. To access share capital the owner would first have to incorporate the business. Until then, the realistic sources are personal funds, retained profit, bank loans, overdrafts, trade credit, leasing, hire purchase, crowdfunding, microfinance or government grants — but not share capital.

faq.q: What factors decide which source of finance a business should choose?
faq.a: Six factors do most of the work. Cost — interest, dividends, fees and total cost over time. Purpose — what the money is for and how long that asset will last. Amount — small sums suit internal or short-term sources, large sums may need share or loan capital. Legal structure — only companies can issue share capital. Level of risk — riskier ventures may be shut out of bank lending and turn to equity or grants. And gearing/control — debt raises gearing and financial risk but keeps ownership, while equity avoids repayments but dilutes control. A strong answer weighs these against the specific business rather than reciting them.

sections.content: No business runs on ideas alone; it runs on cash. It needs money to start up, money to cover the daily gap between paying suppliers and being paid by customers, and money to grow. But finance can be raised in very different ways, and each carries its own cost, risk and consequence for who controls the business. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 3.2 — the internal sources (retained profit, sale of assets, sale and leaseback, personal funds), the external sources (share capital, loan capital, overdrafts, trade credit, leasing, hire purchase, crowdfunding, business angels, venture capital, microfinance and government grants), the split between short-term and long-term finance, and the factors that decide the choice. Because 3.2 questions reward judgement, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a recommendation matched to purpose, amount and risk outscores a memorised list every time.
The core split: internal versus external finance

The first question to ask about any source of finance is where the money comes from. Internal finance is raised from within the business itself, using resources it already has. External finance is injected from outside — from lenders, investors, suppliers or the government. Internal sources are generally cheaper and involve no loss of control, but the amount available is capped by how profitable and asset-rich the business is. External sources can supply far larger sums, but usually carry a cost, a repayment obligation, or a dilution of ownership. Established businesses almost always use a blend of the two.

items: **Retained profit:** profit kept after tax and dividends and reinvested. Internal and long-term; no interest, no dilution of ownership — but only available to profitable firms, and money spent one way cannot be spent another.

**Sale of assets:** selling assets the business no longer needs (surplus machinery, vehicles, land). Turns idle assets into cash, but can only be done once per asset and may be slow to sell at a fair price.

**Sale and leaseback:** selling an asset the business still uses — typically its premises — and leasing it straight back. Releases the cash tied up in the asset while keeping its use, at the cost of ongoing lease payments and lost ownership.

**Personal funds (owner's capital):** the owner's own savings put into the business. Cheap and immediate — the mainstay for sole traders and partnerships — but limited to the owner's wealth and puts that wealth at risk.

sections.content: External sources are more numerous, and it helps to group them by what they actually are: borrowing (debt), selling ownership (equity), supplier arrangements, and specialist or public funding. Each answers a different need.

items: **Share capital:** money raised by selling shares. External, long-term EQUITY — never repaid, no interest — but dilutes ownership and control, and only companies (Ltd/plc) can use it.
**Loan capital:** a lump sum borrowed and repaid over a set term with interest. DEBT finance — keeps ownership intact but adds fixed repayments, usually needs collateral, and raises gearing.
**Overdraft:** a facility to withdraw more than the account holds, up to a limit. Short-term and flexible, interest only on what is used — but high rates and repayable on demand.
**Trade credit:** buying now and paying the supplier later (30–90 days). Short-term and interest-free working capital, but limited in size and costly to supplier goodwill if abused.
**Leasing:** paying to use an asset owned by a lessor, never owning it. Avoids a big upfront outlay and often bundles maintenance, but total cost can exceed buying.
**Hire purchase:** a deposit plus instalments, ending in OWNERSHIP of the asset. Spreads the cost, but instalments carry interest and the asset can be repossessed if payments stop.
**Crowdfunding:** many small contributions raised online for rewards, pre-orders or equity. Raises finance and tests demand at once, but can fail publicly and expose the idea.
**Business angels:** wealthy individuals investing their own money plus expertise into start-ups for equity. Finance and mentoring, but a share of ownership goes with it.
**Venture capital:** specialist firms investing larger, pooled sums into high-growth businesses for equity and influence. Big money, but real loss of control and pressure to grow fast.
**Microfinance:** small loans for low-income entrepreneurs shut out of mainstream banks. Widens access to finance, but sums are small and interest can be high.
**Government grants:** sums given (not lent) to encourage activity such as job creation or R&D. No repayment, but competitive, condition-heavy and usually partial.

sections.question: Distinguish between an internal and an external source of finance, using an example of each. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
An internal source of finance is money raised from within the business itself, without involving any outside party. For example, retained profit — the profit a business keeps after paying tax and dividends — can be reinvested to fund new equipment. Because the money already belongs to the business, it carries no interest and does not dilute ownership.

An external source of finance is money obtained from an individual or institution outside the business. For example, loan capital is a lump sum borrowed from a bank and repaid over a set period with interest. It can provide a much larger amount than the business could generate internally, but it creates a repayment obligation and adds to the firm's financial risk.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — the command term 'distinguish' asks for a clear difference plus a valid example on each side:

- **AO1 (1):** correct meaning of an internal source — raised from within the business.
- **AO2 (1):** a valid, developed example — retained profit reinvested, with a consequence (no interest / no dilution).
- **AO1 (1):** correct meaning of an external source — obtained from outside the business.
- **AO2 (1):** a valid, developed example — loan capital repaid with interest, with a consequence (larger sum but repayment/risk).

The engine credits the CONTRAST, not two definitions in isolation: an answer that defines both terms but never makes clear that one comes from inside and the other from outside is capped below full marks. Naming a source without saying what it is (e.g. writing only 'retained profit') earns the AO1 point but forfeits the developed AO2 example mark.

sections.content: Matching the term of finance to its purpose

Sources of finance are also classified by how long the money is needed. The golden rule is to match the term of the finance to the life of what it funds. Short-term finance — up to about a year — covers day-to-day working-capital needs, such as paying wages or bridging the gap before customers pay. Long-term finance — several years or more — funds major, long-lived assets such as premises, machinery or expansion. Getting this match wrong is expensive: funding a twenty-year factory with a repayable-on-demand overdraft means the debt falls due long before the factory has earned its cost, while using long-term share capital to plug a one-week cash gap needlessly dilutes ownership.

items: **Short-term finance (up to ~1 year):** manages working capital and cash flow. Sources: overdraft, trade credit, short bank loans. Flexible and fast, but usually higher-cost per pound and quickly repayable.

**Long-term finance (several years+):** funds long-lived assets and expansion. Sources: share capital, long-term loans, leasing, hire purchase, retained profit. Larger and more stable, but commits the business for years.
**The matching principle:** short-lived need → short-term source; long-lived asset → long-term source. A mismatch either forces early repayment or over-commits the business.

sections.content: A recurring strategic choice runs beneath these sources: debt versus equity. Debt finance (loans, overdrafts, hire purchase) must be repaid with interest whether or not the business is profitable, so it raises financial risk — but it keeps ownership in the founders' hands. Equity finance (share capital, business angels, venture capital) never has to be repaid and pays no fixed interest, but every new investor takes a slice of ownership and a say in decisions. The balance between the two is captured by gearing: the proportion of capital that comes from debt. Highly geared firms carry heavy fixed interest costs and are vulnerable if profits or sales fall; low-geared firms are safer but may be leaving cheap borrowing — and faster growth — on the table. Gearing therefore sits at the heart of the trade-off between risk and control.

items: **Debt finance:** repaid with interest regardless of profit; keeps ownership; raises gearing and financial risk. Cheaper than equity when profits are strong and stable.
**Equity finance:** no repayment, no fixed interest; dilutes ownership and control; lowers gearing. Safer in downturns but shares future profits.

**Gearing:** high gearing = heavy reliance on debt = higher fixed costs and risk; low gearing = safer but possibly under-using cheap finance. The right level depends on the stability of the industry and cash flows.

sections.content: The factors influencing the choice of source

There is no universally best source of finance — the right one depends on the business and its situation. Six factors do most of the work in the exam. Cost: the interest, dividends, fees and total cost over time. Purpose: what the finance is for, and how long that asset will last. Amount: small sums suit internal or short-term sources, large sums may need share or loan capital. Legal structure: only incorporated companies can issue share capital, which rules it out for sole traders and partnerships. Level of risk: a risky venture or a firm with no trading history may be refused bank lending and pushed toward equity or grants. And gearing/control: debt raises gearing and risk but preserves ownership, while equity avoids repayments but hands over control. A strong answer weighs these factors against the specific business and commits to a justified choice.

items: **Cost:** compare interest, dividends and fees, and the total cost over the life of the finance — the cheapest headline rate is not always the cheapest overall.
**Purpose:** match the source to what the money buys and how long it lasts — working capital versus a long-lived asset.
**Amount:** internal and short-term sources suit small needs; large needs may require share capital or a substantial loan.
**Legal structure:** share capital is open only to companies (Ltd/plc); sole traders and partnerships rely on personal funds, loans, leasing and the like.
**Level of risk:** start-ups and high-risk ventures may be unable to secure loans and turn instead to equity, crowdfunding, angels or grants.
**Gearing/control:** debt keeps control but raises gearing and financial risk; equity lowers risk but dilutes ownership — the decisive factor is often how much control the owners will surrender.

sections.content: Anchor every source recommendation to the case study's own details — the amount needed, what it is for, the firm's legal structure and its appetite for risk. 'As a sole trader with no shares to sell, share capital is not an option, so...' or 'because the vans will last several years, a short-term overdraft is the wrong match...' shows the examiner you are applying the factors, not reciting them. The application marks live in that link between the source and THIS business.

sections.question: A profitable manufacturer needs $600,000 to build a new warehouse expected to last 25 years. It is considering a long-term bank loan or issuing new share capital. Analyse how gearing and control might influence this choice. [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Because the warehouse is a long-lived asset, both options are appropriately long-term, so the decision turns on gearing and control rather than the term of finance.

Taking the $600,000 as a long-term bank loan would raise the manufacturer's gearing, because a larger share of its capital would come from debt. The advantage is that ownership stays entirely with the current owners — no control is given away — and, as the firm is already profitable, it should comfortably meet the interest payments, and interest is a tax-deductible cost. The drawback is higher financial risk: the interest and repayments are fixed and must be paid even if sales fall, so if demand for its products drops the firm could struggle to service a now highly geared balance sheet.

Issuing new share capital instead would lower gearing, because the money comes from equity rather than debt, and there are no fixed repayments to meet in a downturn — which makes the firm more resilient. However, selling new shares dilutes the existing owners' control: new shareholders gain votes and a claim on future profits through dividends, and if enough shares are sold the founders' influence over decisions weakens.

So the choice is a trade-off: the loan protects control but raises gearing and risk, while share capital reduces risk but surrenders some control. For a firm that is already profitable with stable cash flows, the higher gearing from a loan is more manageable, and keeping control may tip the balance toward borrowing — but a firm worried about a possible downturn might prefer the safety of equity.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks** — 'analyse' calls for developed, two-sided reasoning applied to this business:

- **AO1 — knowledge:** correct command of gearing (share of capital from debt) and control (ownership/votes), and that loans are debt while share capital is equity.
- **AO2 — application:** tying the concepts to THIS firm — the $600,000, the 25-year warehouse, the fact that the manufacturer is already profitable.
- **AO3 — analysis:** developed cause-and-effect chains on BOTH sides — the loan raises gearing and fixed-repayment risk but preserves control; share capital lowers gearing and risk but dilutes control — showing the trade-off rather than listing points.

The engine rewards the linked reasoning ('higher gearing → fixed repayments → risk if sales fall'), not the bare terms. An answer that defines gearing and control but never connects them to the warehouse decision is held in the lower bands.

items: **Recommending share capital to a sole trader or partnership** — only incorporated companies (Ltd/plc) can issue shares. Suggesting share capital to an unincorporated business is a factual error that undermines the whole answer.

**Confusing an overdraft with a loan** — an overdraft is short-term, flexible and repayable on demand with interest only on what is used; a loan is a fixed lump sum repaid over a set term with scheduled interest. They suit different purposes and are not interchangeable.

**Mismatching the term of finance to the purpose** — funding a long-lived asset (premises, machinery) with a short-term source like an overdraft, or using long-term share capital to plug a brief cash-flow gap. Match short-term sources to short-term needs and long-term sources to long-lived assets.

**Treating retained profit or grants as 'free' with no downside** — retained profit is limited to profitable firms and, once spent, is unavailable elsewhere (an opportunity cost); grants are competitive, conditional and usually only partial. Neither is a limitless free lunch.

**Confusing leasing with hire purchase** — with hire purchase the business owns the asset after the final instalment; with leasing it only ever pays to use the asset and never owns it. State which one actually transfers ownership.

**Confusing debt and equity, or business angels and venture capital** — loans are debt (repaid with interest, no ownership lost); share capital is equity (no repayment, ownership diluted). Angels invest their own money in early-stage start-ups; venture capital firms invest larger pooled sums in proven high-growth firms.

**Listing advantages and disadvantages without applying them** — a bare list of pros and cons earns AO1 only. Marks climb when each point is tied to the specific business, its amount, purpose and risk.

**Recommending without a supported judgement** — a 'recommend' or 'evaluate' answer that gives both sides but never commits to a justified conclusion, matched to purpose, amount and risk, cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 3.2 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge of the sources of finance, AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business in the stimulus, and AO3 rewards analysis and a balanced evaluation. In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit, but the higher 'recommend' and 'evaluate' marks are reserved for answers that combine APPLICATION to context with a BALANCED evaluation that ends in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT matched to purpose, amount and risk. Watch how the marks below attach to applied, two-sided reasoning and a justified conclusion — never to a generic list.

sections.question: Recommend the most appropriate source of finance for a sole trader wishing to buy new delivery vans. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The sole trader needs finance for a specific, long-lived asset — delivery vans that will be used for several years — so the source should be long- or medium-term, and, crucially, because the business is a sole trader it cannot issue share capital: only incorporated companies can sell shares. That immediately rules out equity and focuses the choice on sources open to an unincorporated business. Three realistic options stand out: a bank loan, leasing (or hire purchase), and retained profit or personal funds.

A bank loan matches the purpose well. The vans are a durable asset, so a medium-term loan repaid over, say, three to five years spreads the cost across the years the vans are earning revenue, and the owner keeps full ownership of both the vans and the business — no control is given away. The drawbacks are that the loan must be repaid with interest whether or not deliveries are profitable, it raises the sole trader's financial risk, and the bank will likely want the vans (or personal assets) as collateral — a real exposure given the owner already has unlimited liability.

Leasing, or hire purchase, is an attractive alternative for vehicles specifically. Leasing avoids a large upfront outlay, spreads payments, and often bundles in maintenance and easy upgrades to newer, more reliable vans — valuable for a delivery business that depends on vehicles being on the road. The downside is that with a lease the trader never owns the vans and the total cost over time can exceed buying outright; hire purchase fixes the ownership problem, since the trader owns the vans after the final instalment, but still spreads interest-bearing payments and risks repossession if payments are missed.

Retained profit or the owner's personal funds would be the cheapest option — no interest, no loss of control — but a sole trader's internal funds are usually limited, and sinking savings into vans drains the reserves the business may need for day-to-day cash flow. Using all internal cash on a single purchase is risky for a small firm.

**Judgement.** On balance, the most appropriate source is a medium-term bank loan (or hire purchase if the trader wants the ease of vehicle finance), because it matches the term of finance to the several-year life of the vans, preserves the owner's full control of the business, and spreads the cost across the period the vans generate income. Share capital is not available to a sole trader, and relying wholly on limited personal funds would leave the business dangerously short of working capital. The main drawback — interest and repayment risk — is manageable because the vans will directly generate the delivery revenue used to repay the loan, and it is a price worth paying to keep control while acquiring an asset the business genuinely needs. If, however, the trader values flexibility and up-to-date vehicles over ownership, leasing would be the stronger choice — but for acquiring an owned, revenue-earning asset at reasonable cost, the bank loan is the appropriate recommendation.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 — knowledge (sources concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the relevant sources — loan capital as debt, leasing versus hire purchase (use versus eventual ownership), retained profit and personal funds as internal sources, and the fact that share capital is closed to a sole trader. Correct terminology and the internal/external and short/long-term distinctions signal AO1.
- **AO2 — application to THIS business and purpose:** marks are awarded for tying those sources to the sole trader buying delivery vans specifically — the vans as a several-year asset that suits medium-term finance, the vans generating the revenue that repays a loan, the owner's unlimited liability making collateral a real exposure, and share capital being ruled out because there are no shares to sell. Generic points that never mention the vans or the sole-trader structure do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — balanced analysis of cost, control and risk + supported judgement:** the engine credits developed weighing of two or three suitable sources — a loan (control kept, but interest and repayment risk) against leasing/hire purchase (flexibility and maintenance, but no ownership or higher total cost) against retained/personal funds (cheapest, but drains limited reserves) — and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to one source and justifies WHY it fits the purpose, amount and risk better than the alternatives.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus a matched judgement. The engine rewards an answer that weighs suitable sources against the vans and the sole-trader context and ends in a reasoned recommendation matched to purpose, amount and risk; it does NOT reward a longer generic list of every source's pros and cons.
- **What the engine rejects:** recommending share capital to a sole trader (a factual error); listing sources with no application to the vans; a 'judgement' that restates both sides without choosing; and ignoring the matching principle by proposing a short-term overdraft for a multi-year asset.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the right sources concepts, AO2 for applying them to the business and its purpose, and AO3 for balanced analysis of cost, control and risk ending in a supported judgement. So do not just list sources: identify the ones actually available to this business (remember share capital is companies-only), weigh two or three against the purpose, amount and risk, and finish by committing to a recommendation with a reason matched to what the money is for. The application and the matched judgement are where the top-band marks are won.

The choices in this topic feed directly into the rest of the finance and accounts unit. The internal/external and debt/equity distinctions underpin costs and revenues and the final accounts, where loan interest, share capital and retained profit all appear; gearing links forward to profitability and liquidity ratio analysis; and the matching of finance to purpose returns in cash-flow forecasting and investment appraisal. Master the habit built here — identify which sources the business can actually use, match them to the purpose, amount and risk, weigh cost and control, then commit to a justified judgement — and you have the template that earns marks across every finance evaluation question in Business Management.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: recommend the most appropriate source of finance for a business, applying the concepts and reaching a supported judgement matched to purpose, amount and risk
